segments.cost: Cabin: $10-$20 for this segment. Deck passage: $2-$4 (1840s)
segments.conditions: Deck passengers sleep among cotton bales, barrels of molasses, and hogsheads of sugar on the lower deck, inches above the waterline and directly over the boilers. The heat is suffocating — the furnaces burn day and night to drive the paddlewheels against the current. Sparks drift over the cargo. Enslaved people being transported for sale occupy the same deck space, chained or under guard, a fact that cabin passengers above can see but choose not to discuss.

segments.cost: included in through-passage; deck passengers may purchase food from vendors at landings or go hungry
segments.conditions: The river bends through the Mississippi Delta bottomlands, a landscape of cottonwood, cypress, and mosquito-thick swamp. The pilot navigates by memory and instinct — the channel shifts with every flood, and submerged snags can rip through a hull in seconds. At plantation landings, the boat takes on cotton and sometimes takes on enslaved people being sold downriver to the sugar parishes. Memphis appears as a bluff town smelling of hardwood smoke and river mud.

segments.distance: approximately 200 miles to Cairo, 400 miles to St. Louis
segments.travel_time: 3-4 days upstream to St. Louis (1840s), 2 days downstream
segments.cost: Cabin: $25-$50 total New Orleans to St. Louis. Deck passage: $5-$8 total
segments.conditions: At Cairo, the Ohio River joins the Mississippi and the boat enters the Upper River — narrower, faster current, bluffs replacing bayou. Fuel stops every few hours where roustabouts — often free Black men or hired-out enslaved men — carry cordwood aboard at a dead run. Boiler explosions are shockingly common; one estimate counts 500 or more boats lost between 1810 and 1850. The Sultana disaster of April 1865 killed over 1,168 passengers, mostly Union soldiers returning from Confederate prison camps.

segments.to: Cincinnati, Ohio (via Ohio River) / Kansas City, Missouri (via Missouri River)
segments.distance: approximately 600 miles to Cincinnati via Ohio, 400 miles to Kansas City via Missouri
segments.travel_time: 4-6 days to Cincinnati, 5-7 days to Kansas City (1840s-1850s)
segments.conditions: The Ohio River branch carries German and Irish immigrants upstream toward Cincinnati, the 'Queen City' that smells of pork processing and beer brewing. The Missouri River branch is shallower, wilder, and more dangerous — snags and sandbars ground boats daily, and passengers headed to Kansas City or Omaha accept delays as part of the journey. Both branches serve as feeders for overland trails heading further west.

who_used_it: German and Irish immigrants heading to St. Louis, Cincinnati, and the upper Midwest (1830s-1860s). Enslaved people forcibly transported from the Upper South to the Deep South cotton and sugar plantations via the domestic slave trade — New Orleans received an estimated 100,000 enslaved people by river between 1820 and 1860. Appalachian families moving west to Missouri and Kansas. Returning Union soldiers and prisoners of war (1865). The route carried dual significance: freedom and opportunity for some, forced removal and sale for others.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} likely traveled the Mississippi by steamboat between {origin} and {destination} — riding the great highway of antebellum America where immigrants, cargo, and enslaved people shared the same deck, and every departure carried the knowledge that boiler explosions could end any journey without warning.
source_references: Walter Johnson, 'River of Dark Dreams: Slavery and Empire in the Cotton Kingdom' (Harvard University Press, 2013)
Louis C. Hunter, 'Steamboats on the Western Rivers: An Economic and Technological History' (Harvard University Press, 1949)
Adam Rothman, 'Slave Country: American Expansion and the Origins of the Deep South' (Harvard University Press, 2005)
Michael Allen, 'Western Rivermen, 1763-1861: Ohio and Mississippi Boatmen and the Myth of the Alligator Horse' (Louisiana State University Press, 1990)
